posts.name: A Ballin
posts.cooked:
I found a small wine bar close to home specializing in natural wine. I am partial to a glass or two of natural juice and Overnoy has always been a favorite, however, I have experienced a lot of bottle variation in the past.

Tonight the 2012 Poulsard was awesome, wild, strawberries, crisp and cool, this is a wine to drink and enjoy laughter with friends. Super. Drink now is my call although friends suggest they do age.

The 2005 Chardonnay, was dense and rich, super interesting with layers of complexity. Waxy, orchard fruits and a touch of lemon. Great drinking right now.

If you see these wines, buy them and enjoy them. Will post a picture tomorrow.

posts.name: A Ballin
posts.cooked: Easier to find in Tokyo than elsewhere I guess…

posts.name: paul clark
posts.cooked:
A Ballin:

I found a small wine bar close to home specializing in natural wine. I am partial to a glass or two of natural juice and Overnoy has always been a favorite, however, I have experienced a lot of bottle variation in the past.

Tonight the 2012 Poulsard was awesome, wild, strawberries, crisp and cool, this is a wine to drink and enjoy laughter with friends. Super. Drink now is my call although friends suggest they do age.

The 2005 Chardonnay, was dense and rich, super interesting with layers of complexity. Waxy, orchard fruits and a touch of lemon. Great drinking right now.

If you see these wines, buy them and enjoy them. Will post a picture tomorrow.

Thanks for the notes. I have had “good luck” with regards to consistency between bottles. I enjoy their non-ox’d cuvées as much as some of the other top producers in the region. I find their more oxidised cuvées are head and shoulders above the rest; especially their Vieux Savagnin Ouillé. I have one bottle of 99 vin jaune in the cave that I picked up a little over a year ago and, considering the effort invested in finding one of those damned things, I am saving it for a very special occasion.

posts.name: A Ballin
posts.cooked:
Wow…The Vin Jaunes are almost impossible to find…that would be a long term hold for me and awaiting a baked Mont Dor.

I have had issues in the past with secondary fermentation in the poulsard.

posts.name: paul clark
posts.cooked:
Interesting to know on 2eme fermentation in the poulsard.

Here in Geneva / Lausanne I can get the Chardonnays without any problem. The VSO is relatively easy to track down and I suspect that is a function of the cost (+/-$85 for a 50cL bottle).

The Pupillin Rouge is a lot more difficult to get hands on. The Pupillin Savagnin is about as easy to get a hold of as the Rouge.

I’ve only seen one bottle of the Vin Jaune and I bought the MF’r. Same shop where I bought 09/10 mags of Chave for $350 each. I put a LOT of time in at that shop to be on the short list for those bottles and, since, just haven’t had the time (or desire) to keep it up.

posts.name: Jay Miller
posts.cooked:
Chris Tuttle:

Yeah, if you see these wines…

You said it.

I remember when they just sat on the shelf at Garnet and I could walk in and pick up a bottle whenever I wanted to. Now I’m not a big enough balla to get an allocation.

posts.name: Ramon Cabrera
posts.cooked:
That 2005 Chardonnay continues to be an enigma. It was not as positively received by most during a dinner here in NYC, but a few seem to like it.

I wish I can have the opportunity to revisit another bottle, but that’s easier said than done with these wines.

posts.name: Michae1 P0wers
posts.cooked:
Jay Miller:
Chris Tuttle:

Yeah, if you see these wines…

You said it.

I remember when they just sat on the shelf at Garnet and I could walk in and pick up a bottle whenever I wanted to. Now I’m not a big enough balla to get an allocation.

Kind of like Pappy van Winkle. Makes you wonder what is just sitting on store shelves today that will be unattainable tomorrow.

posts.name: Jay Miller
posts.cooked:
R Cabrera:

That 2005 Chardonnay continues to be an enigma. It was not as positively received by most during a dinner here in NYC, but a few seem to like it.

I wish I can have the opportunity to revisit another bottle, but that’s easier said than done with these wines.

I was never as big a fan of the whites as of the Poulsard. Moot point now of course

Finally had the 2012 poulsard last week as well and decanted it for about 2.5 hours prior to formal drinking. Made a world of difference. The funkiness that was more upfront at initial opening lessened and took more of a backseat to the deep florals and the freshness came through nicely. Would love to see how this one ages, but it certainly did not disappoint.

Have not gone further back than 2009 with his chards yet and have experienced some bottle variation. I would imagine that the 2005 would just be coming in to its prime now. Your notes sound like what others have said regarding not opening the chards too early as it pays off.

The VJ is a huge pain to source and I too only have one bottle so no rush opening that beast just yet!

posts.name: Faryan Amir-Ghassemi
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:

Agreed, the 2005 Chardonnay is spectacular!

Also loved the wine when I had it in Paris in '13 but found it so dense and needing of time to really flesh out.

posts.name: Michael Lewis
posts.cooked:
The 2005 Chard needs to be decanted these days (what Overnoy doesn’t?), but I have had some bottles that really got going after a few hours.

The 2012 Poulsard is much more structured than other recent vintages of the Poulsard. Manu Houillon told me last March that it reminded him of some of the more tannic, structured vintages that Pierre made in the 1990s. It should age wonderfully.

Thank you for the details Michael!

I’ve pretty much always decanted Overnoys, but not long enough until recently. Feel like I’ve been doing them a disservice -_-*

Nice to get some thoughts from Houillon himself regarding the 12 poulsard. Need to source out more to put away!
